coredata.dc:title: Color-multiplexing europium doped carbon dots for highly selective and dosage-sensitive cascade visualization of tetracycline and Al3+
coredata.dc:creator: Sang, Yuxin
Wang, Kun
Kong, Xiangping
Cheng, Fansheng
Zhou, Chuan
Li, Weina
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report a dosage-sensitive europium doped carbon dots (Eu-CDs) with a wide and consecutive color variation from blue to red and reversely from red to blue response to the cascade presence of tetracycline (TC) and aluminum ions (Al3+). With the dosage variation of TC, the fluorescence color of Eu-CDs gradually changes from blue to light purple to final red. The fluorescence intensity ratio of I620/I468 exhibits a linear relationship in the range of 0–100 μM with a detection limit (LOD) of 6.9 nM. The cascade addition of different concentration of Al3+ could reversely change the fluorescence color from red to pink to final blue. A good linear relationship of I468/I620 in the concentration range of 0–50 μM with a LOD of 28.6 nM was obtained. The smartphone-assisted point-of-care testing platform was constructed and displayed dosage scale as low as 13.2 nM and 160 nM for TC and Al3+, respectively. This strategy was further successfully used for on-site visual detection of TC and Al3+ in real samples with satisfied recovery ranged from 93.30% to 108.88%. The strategy proposes a bright future of fluorescent colorimetric sensors for the extensive applications in the assays of environments, medicines, and foods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tetracycline
Europium doped carbon dots
Dosage sensitive sensor
Color variation
Cascade assay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of TC and Al3+ detection conditions
Principle of Eu-CDs for cascade detection of TC and Al3+
Cascade detection of TC and Al3+
Selectivity of Eu-CDs towards TC and Al3+
Smartphone-based visual detection of TC and Al3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of Eu-CDs (inset: HRTEM image of Eu-CDs with lattice parameters of 0.20 nm). (b) Size distribution histogram of Eu-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Eu-CDs. (b) Excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of Eu-CDs. Inset: photographs of Eu-CDs under visible light and UV light (365 nm), respectively. (c) Fluorescence decay curves of Eu-CDs and Eu-CDs+TC.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of Eu-CDs with the addition of different concentrations of TC. (b) The linear relationships between F620/F468 and TC concentration. (c) The corresponding fluorescent photographs taken under a 365 nm UV lamp.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of Eu-CDs-TC with the addition of different concentrations of Al3+. (b) The linear relationships between F468/F620 and Al3+ concentration. (c) The corresponding fluorescent photographs taken under a 365 nm UV lamp.

Fluorescence responses of Eu-CDs toward different kinds of (a) cations and (c) antibiotics. The corresponding fluorescent photographs of Eu-CDs toward different kinds of (b) cations and (d) antibiotics under a 365 nm UV lamp. (e) Fluorescence responses of Eu-CDs-TC toward different kinds of cations and (f) the corresponding fluorescent photographs under a 365 nm UV lamp.

(a) Photographs of test paper loaded with Eu-CDs under daylight and UV lamp before and after addition of 5 μL of TC solution with diverse concentrations. (b) Fitting curve between the concentration of TC and R/B ratio. (c) Photographs of test paper loaded with Eu-CDs-TC under daylight and UV lamp before and after addition of 5 μL of Al3+ solution with diverse concentrations. (d) Fitting curve between the concentration of Al3+ and B/R ratio.
Schematic illustration of synthesis Eu-CDs and the cascade dosage sensitive colorimetric sensing of TC and Al3+.

head.ce:title: Color-multiplexing europium doped carbon dots for highly selective and dosage-sensitive cascade visualization of tetracycline and Al3+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao, 266109, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian, Research Institute of Chemical Defense, Beijing 102205, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian, Research Institute of Chemical Defense

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian, Research Institute of Chemical Defense, Beijing 102205, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Qingdao Agricultural University, 700 Changcheng Road, Qingdao 266109, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report a dosage-sensitive europium doped carbon dots (Eu-CDs) with a wide and consecutive color variation from blue to red and reversely from red to blue response to the cascade presence of tetracycline (TC) and aluminum ions (Al3+). With the dosage variation of TC, the fluorescence color of Eu-CDs gradually changes from blue to light purple to final red. The fluorescence intensity ratio of I620/I468 exhibits a linear relationship in the range of 0–100 μM with a detection limit (LOD) of 6.9 nM. The cascade addition of different concentration of Al3+ could reversely change the fluorescence color from red to pink to final blue. A good linear relationship of I468/I620 in the concentration range of 0–50 μM with a LOD of 28.6 nM was obtained. The smartphone-assisted point-of-care testing platform was constructed and displayed dosage scale as low as 13.2 nM and 160 nM for TC and Al3+, respectively. This strategy was further successfully used for on-site visual detection of TC and Al3+ in real samples with satisfied recovery ranged from 93.30% to 108.88%. The strategy proposes a bright future of fluorescent colorimetric sensors for the extensive applications in the assays of environments, medicines, and foods.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantitative analysis of target objects has been extensively implemented by using traditional laboratory instruments, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [1], mass spectrometry (MS) [2] and electrochemical techniques [3,4]. These techniques are unsuitable for on-site assays due to the requirement of expensive instrumentations, professional technicians, and complicated pretreatment. It is urgent to explore a fast, simple and portable strategy for the on-site assay of target species in foods, medicines, and environments.

Tetracycline (TC), a broad-spectrum antibiotic, has been extensively applied in human therapy, animal husbandry and aquaculture. It is estimated that about 5000 metric tons of TC are consumed annually [5]. The abuse of TC as feed additive in animal breeding result in antibiotic residues in animal-origin foodstuffs, such as meat, milk, egg, fish and honey [6], which can provoke the rise of drug-resistant bacteria and cause serious threat to human health, leading to anaphylactic reaction, hepatotoxicity and gastrointestinal disorders [7,8]. To ensure human food safety, the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and European Union (EU) have established the maximum residue limits in animal products to be 676 nM and 225 nM, respectively.

Aluminum (Al) is the third abundant element in the earth’s crust and has been widely used in water treatment, cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, and food additives [9]. The increasing exposure to aluminum is considered to be a possible cause of Parkinson’s disease, Alzheimer’s disease, renal osteodystropy, and even breast cancer [10,11]. The World Health Organization established a maximum permissible content of Al3+ ion in drinking water is 7.41 μM [12]. It is interesting to develop facile and accurate approach with the capability of simultaneously quantitative assay of TC and Al3+ in foods and environments.

Fluorescence methods with fluorescence turn-on [13], turn-off [14], or ratiometric responses to the target species provide an ideal alternative owing to their sensitivity and simplicity. Especially, the ratiometric fluorescent measurements, which possess two self-referencing emission peaks [15], can provide more accurate visual quantification through color changes than only through fluorescence brightness “turn on” or “turn off” [16,17]. However, the development of multicolor fluorescent probes possessing wide color variations with the dosages of more than one target remains great challenge and is not fully implemented.

Herein, we present a fluorescent colorimetric sensor with a wide and consecutive color variations with the target dosages for cascade quantification of TC and Al3+ by the naked eye observation of fluorescence color change. Firstly, blue emission Eu-CDs were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method with citric acid, melamine and formaldehyde as raw materials and Eu(NO3)3.6H2O as Eu dopant. The Eu-CDs can simultaneously serve as the interior fluorescence reference and response unit for TC and Al3+. Upon changing the concentration of TC, the fluorescence intensity ratio of I620/I468 exhibits a good linear relationship to TC concentrations ranged from 0 to 200 μM with a LOD of 6.9 nM. Concomitantly, the emission color under a UV lamp changed from blue to brownish yellow to light purple to light rose red to pink to final red. The cascade addition of Al3+ to Eu-CDs-TC systems could reversely change the fluorescence intensity ratio of I620/I468 in the concentration range of 0–50 μM with a LOD of 28.6 nM. The corresponding fluorescence color changed from red to rose red to pink to light yellow to final blue, revealing a dosage sensitive visualization of TC and Al3+. Moreover, when the Eu-CDs solution was printed onto the filter paper, the similar color evolution was obtained for TC and Al3+. Combining color scanning APP, the smartphone-assisted point-of-care testing platform was also constructed and displayed the dosage scale as low as 13.2 nM and 160 nM for TC and Al3+, respectively. This strategy was further successfully used for visual detection of TC and Al3+ in real samples with satisfied recovery ranged from 93.30% to 108.88%. This one-step hydrothermal method prepared.

Eu-CDs with wide and consecutive color variations successfully achieved the dosage sensitive on-site detection of TC and Al3+. This platform presents a great promise for visual on-site assay of different targets in practical application with merits of simple preparation and operation, easy to carry, remarkable selectivity and repeatability and proposes a bright future of fluorescent colorimetric sensors for the extensive applications in the assays of environments, medicines, and foods.

Eu-CDs were synthesized by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method using citric acid, melamine and Eu(NO3)3.6H2O as the major carbon, nitrogen and europium source ( Scheme 1), which simultaneously incorporated Eu element (TC-bonding site) and luminescent CDs (fluorophores). Formaldehyde was added as passivating agent to modify the chemical state of nitrogen in Eu-CDs [18]. As shown in Fig. 1a, Eu-CDs are uniform distributed spherical nanoparticles with diameters ranging from 1.0 to 3.0 nm, and the average size is 2.0 nm (Fig. 1b). A further close observation from HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1a) shows that Eu-CDs present obvious crystal lattice fringes with lattice spacing of 0.20 nm, which is in accordance with the (100) facet of graphite carbon [19]. Elemental mapping confirmed the presence of C, N, O and Eu, and all the elements were uniformly-distributed throughout the Eu-CDs (Fig. S1).

FTIR spectrum (Fig. S2a) shows broad peaks at 3000–3600 cm−1, which can be attributed to the O-H/N-H stretching vibrations [20]. Peaks at 1675 cm−1, 1574 cm−1, 1452 cm−1 and 1262 cm−1 can be assigned to the CO, CC, C-N and C-O stretching vibration, respectively. The XPS full scan spectrum displays four elements, C, N, O and Eu with contents of 60.11, 2.99, 33.03 and 3.87 at% (Fig. S2b). Five dominant peaks at 281.08, 398.08, 537.08, 1134.0 and 1164.0 are observed, which are corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s, and Eu3d. The high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. S3a) shows three main peaks at 284.7, 285.7 and 288.4 eV, which are ascribed to the C-C/CC, C-N/C-O and CO bonds. XPS analysis of the N1s spectrum (Fig. S3b) shows two peaks at 399.9 and 401.5 eV, corresponding to N-C and N-H groups. The O1s spectrum (Fig. S3c) consists of three peaks at 531.2, 532.0 and 532.9 eV, which are attributed to CO and C-OH groups. The Eu3d region (Fig. S3d) shows two sets of peaks at around 1134.0 and 1164.0 eV, which are assigned to the photoemission from 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 structures [21]. The high resolution Eu3d spectrum consists four peaks, which are ascribed to Eu3+ (1165.0 and 1135.4 eV) and Eu2+ (1155.0 and 1125.5 eV), demonstrating the coexistence of Eu3+ and Eu2+. All the results demonstrate the existence of Eu element and the presence of abundant oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of Eu-CDs, which renders Eu-CDs with excellent water-solubility.

UV–vis spectrum ( Fig. 2a) displays obvious absorption peak at 220 nm, which is attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic CC [18]. The absorption cross section was calculated to be 0.1036 nm2. Eu-CDs exhibit excitation wavelength dependent emissions with the maximum emission at 420 nm under the excitation of 350 nm (Fig. 2b). The characteristic transition emission peaks of Eu3+ are not observed in the fluorescence spectrum. Under 365 nm UV lamp, Eu-CDs display bright blue emission (inset of Fig. 2b). The average fluorescence lifetime is measured to be 7.24 ns (Fig. 2c, Table S1) and fluorescence quantum yield (QY) is 10.81%. It is further revealed that the fluorescence of Eu-CDs remained stable at pH range 2.0–12.0 (Fig. S4a), in different ionic intensity environments (Fig. S4b), irradiated by UV light for 60 min (Fig. S4c) and for 15 days storage at room temperature. All the results demonstrate that Eu-CDs have excellent fluorescence properties and outstanding fluorescence stability, which is beneficial for TC and Al3+ determination in complex samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of TC and Al3+ detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of heteroatom Eu concentration on the fluorescence intensity was investigated. Fig. S5 shows fluorescence response to TC of Eu-CDs with Eu doping amount varied from 300 to 800 mg and corresponding fluorescence color change. It is revealed that 500 mg of Eu dopant yielded the optimal fluorescence intensity and achieved a more satisfactory visual detection of TC. So the dopant amount 500 mg was selected for further experiments.

The effect of incubation time was also evaluated. As shown in Fig. S6a, the fluorescence intensity ratio F620/F468 significantly increased and reached stable after 0.5 min when Eu-CDs exposed to TC. For the cascade detection of Al3+, the fluorescence intensity ratio F620/F468 decreased gradually with the incubation time of 0–4 min and reached stable value after 4 min (Fig. S6b). Thus, 0.5 min and 4 min were set as the optimal incubation time for TC and Al3+ detection, respectively. The pH value of solution was considered as another critical factor for fluorescence response. It is revealed that 8.0 was the optimal pH and the highest fluorescence intensity ratio was obtained for both TC and Al3+ (Fig. S6c-d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of Eu-CDs for cascade detection of TC and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing principle of Eu-CDs towards TC and Al3+ is demonstrated in Scheme 1. In the absence of TC, Eu-CDs display blue emission. TC contains a β-diketone configuration and can coordinate with Eu3+ to form stable TC-Eu complex to modulate the energy transfer pathways through “antenna effect” [22]. With the addition of TC, the blue luminescence of Eu-CDs quenched, while the red luminescence greatly enhanced owing to the coordination of Eu-CDs and TC [23–25]. The intensity of blue and red emission continuous variation with the concentration of TC lead to the color change from blue to brownish yellow to light purple to light rose red to pink to final red under 365 nm UV light. Afterwards, the cascade addition of Al3+ to Eu-CDs-TC system lead to the dissociation of TC [26] and result the recovery of blue emission and decrease of red emission. Simultaneously, the system color under UV light changes from red to rose red to pink to light yellow to final blue with the dosage of Al3+. Therefore, a colorimetric sensor was fabricated and the cascade quantitative detection of TC and Al3+ was realized.

In the past decades, several Eu3+ modified nanomaterials, including cyclen functionalized CDs [27], SiNPs [28], graphene quantum dots (QDs) [29], CdTe QDs [16], g-C3N4 nanosheets [25], and boron nitride QDs [30] were reported. All the sensing platform were constructed by multistep delicate modification with Eu3+. The reference fluorescent signals of some sensing probes could not change with the enhancement of Eu3+ fluorescence intensity [31], leading to the naked eye observation is unobvious. In the case of Al3+ determination, most fluorescent probes are turn-on or turn-off sensors [32,33], which are lack of accuracy for on-site colorimetric sensing. The assay of Al3+ using concentration dependent fluorescent sensors with wide naked-eye visible color variation is great challenge and urgency. Remarkably, our strategy develops CDs doped with Eu3+ by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. This Eu-CDs can simultaneously serve as the interior fluorescence reference and response unit to realize dosage sensitive visual sensing of TC and Al3+.

The possible mechanism were further explored and confirmed. Firstly, the TC-induced blue emission quenching mechanism was evaluated. As demonstrated in Fig. S7a, the absorption spectrum of TC shows a large spectral overlap with the excitation spectrum of Eu-CDs. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime of Eu-CDs exhibits negligible change from 7.24 to 6.90 ns with the addition of TC (Fig. 2c), demonstrating that there is no energy transfer between Eu-CDs and TC. These results manifested that TC-induced fluorescence quenching of Eu-CDs was attributed to the inner filter effect based steady quenching mechanism [34]. As further demonstrated by the control experiment (Fig. S8a), the fluorescence intensity of CDs without Eu dopant was indeed quenched by TC.

Then the red fluorescence enhancement process of Eu-CDs induced by TC was investigated. As displayed in Fig. S7b, the absorption peaks of Eu-CDs-TC show obvious redshift comparing to that of TC, indicating the chelation of TC with Eu-CDs [35]. Besides, the fluorescence lifetime of dopant Eu in Eu-CDs were detected by using 350 nm wavelength excitation, which was prolonged from 12.15 μs to 46.20 μs with the addition of 100 μM TC (Fig. S9a, S9c, Table S2). This may be ascribed to the fact that TC can replace the complex H2O effectively from the unsaturated position of dopant Eu, which can sensitize the luminescence of Eu via the energy transduction from TC to dopant Eu [36,37]. To achieve optimal energy transfer process from TC to Eu, a suitable energy gap is required between the triplet state energy (T1) of ligand TC and the radiated state of Eu (5D j ). As previously reported, the T1 of TC is determined to be 18100 cm−1 [38], which is well located between the energy of 5D0 (17260 cm−1) and 5D1 (19020 cm−1) of Eu, verifying the occurrence of energy transfer from TC to Eu [39].

Furthermore, the removal of complex H2O is also confirmed. As revealed in Fig. S9 and Table S2, the number of H2O (n
w ) coordinated with the inner sphere of Eu in Eu-CDs and Eu-CDs-TC were determined based on lifetimes of Eu ions in H2O (τH2O) and D2O (τD2O) by the empirical equation: [40].
n
w
= 1.1 [
τ
H 2 O
− 1
−
τ
D 2 O
− 1
− 0.31 ]

The calculated amount of coordinated H2O of Eu-CDs-TC (n
w = 11.01) is far less than that of Eu-CDs (n
w = 85.14), which demonstrated that the coordinated H2O were removed from the inner sphere of Eu in the Eu-CDs after the addition of TC [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cascade detection of TC and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimum conditions, the cascade detection behavior of Eu-CDs towards TC and Al3+ were investigated. Eu-CDs shows blue emission band centered at 420 nm without TC ( Fig. 3a).

Upon addition of TC in the concentration range of 0–100 μM, red emission band at 620 nm appeared and the intensity continuously increased. The original blue emission band at 420 nm red-shifted to 468 nm and the intensity gradually decreased to quenching. The red shift of blue-emission band can be attributed to the band gap transitions of Eu-CDs with the addition of TC [41]. The intensity of red and blue emission continued change leading to apparent color variations, which was changed from blue to brownish yellow to light purple to light rose red to pink to final red under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 3c). To further illustrate the superiority, the sensitivity of CDs without Eu dopant was also determined (Fig. S8a). The blue emission band at 420 nm gradually decreased and no red emission band was observed. Correspondingly, the single blue emission quenching could not cause remarkable color variations (Fig. S8b). The fluorescence ratio F620/F468 displays a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.999) with LOD of 6.9 nM (Fig. 3b), which is well below the allowed concentrations defined by the U.S. FDA (676 nM) and EU (225 nM) [42]. Comparing with previously reported Eu3+-based approach for TC detection (Table S3), our strategy showed comparable detection limits, presented a simple and fast visual on-site quantitative detection potential without special cost apparatus.

The Eu-CDs-TC system was further used for the cascade detection of Al3+. As adding Al3+ with the concentration of 0–50 μM, the red emission band at 620 nm continuously decreased, whereas blue emission band at 468 nm increased gradually and showed blue-shift ( Fig. 4a). Correspondingly, the visible color under 365 nm lamp changes from red to rose red to pink to light yellow to final blue (Fig. 4c). The fluorescence ratio of F468/F620 displays a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.996) with LOD of 28.6 nM (Fig. 4b). Compared with previous reported works (Table S4), this method exhibited low LOD and high sensitivity. And this concomitant fluorescence changes of TC/Al3+ sensing can be clearly identified by the naked eye, even at a TC/Al3+ concentration as low as 5 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of Eu-CDs towards TC and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of Eu-CDs towards TC was elucidated by screening its response to a number of biological ions (Al3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Mn2+) and other antibiotics, including chlortetracycline (CTC), erythromycin (ETC) and oxytetracycline (OTC). As shown in Fig. 5, all the biological ions and other antibiotics have no effects on the fluorescence and color of Eu-CDs. Only TC can induce prominent fluorescence enhancement at 620 nm and distinguished color changes from blue to red (Fig. 5c, d), demonstrating the excellent selectivity toward TC.

The selectivity of Eu-CDs-TC towards Al3+ was also demonstrated. As adding different metal ions, only Al3+ can induce the recovery of blue emission at 468 nm and simultaneously the decrease of red emission at 620 nm (Fig. 5e) to produce apparent color changes from red to blue. The addition of other metal ions showed bright red color under UV lamp (Fig. 5f). It is revealed that Eu-CDs has the great ability to selectively identify Al3+ in cascade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-based visual detection of TC and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: Eu-CDs were printed on test paper and TC with different concentrations were added. Under the irradiation of a UV lamp, the color of test papers changed from blue to brownish yellow to light purple to light rose red to pink to final red. The emission color information (RGB values) were analyzed by a smartphone application (Colorimeter) ( Fig. 6a). As shown in Fig. 6b, the red to blue channel ratio (R/B) showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of TC (R2 = 0.99) in the range of 0–150 μM. The LOD is calculated to be 13.2 nM, which is in accordance with the ratiometric fluorescence result and much lower than FDA and EU allowed concentration. Similarly, for the cascade detection of Al3+, the emission color of test papers changed from red to rose red to pink to light yellow to final blue (Fig. 6c). The corresponding B/R ratio was calculated and showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of Al3+ (R2 = 0.997) in the range of 0–80 μM with LOD of 160.0 nM (Fig. 6d). Eu-CDs can achieve visual and on-site quantitative detection of TC and Al3+ conveniently with a smartphone, which are convenient and practical, especially in remote rural areas.

This approach was also employed in real samples to testify its practical applicability. The milk samples purchased from Qingdao supermarket were spiked with different concentrations of TC and Al3+, respectively. When the spiked milk samples were added on the modified test papers, the corresponding emission color changes were recorded and displayed in Fig. S10. By using both the fluorescence intensity ratio change of F468/F620 and smartphone based color variation method, the recoveries of 93.30–108.88% and relative standard deviations of 1.12–3.26% were obtained (Table S5-S6). The above results strongly illustrated that this proposed approach held great potential for visual and on-site quantitative detection of TC and Al3+ in real samples.

In conclusion, we presented a fluorescent colorimetric sensor with a wide and consecutive color variations for the dosage-sensitive cascade assay of TC and Al3+ by the naked eye with the assistance of smartphone. The blue-emitting Eu-CDs synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method can simultaneously serve as the interior fluorescence reference and response unit for TC and Al3+. This colorimetric sensor realized dosage sensitive multicolor-varying visual cascade sensing for TC and Al3+ without expensive and complicated instrumentation. Taking advantage of the sensitive blue and red fluorescence change, Eu-CDs presents a detection limit of 6.9 nM and 28.6 nM for TC and Al3+, respectively. The good recoveries ranging from 93.30% to 108.88% and low minimum detectable concentrations in real samples were obtained. Eu-CDs exhibit a broad prospect in visual and on-site quantitative detection of TC and Al3+. The results proposed a bright future of fluorescent colorimetric sensors for the extensive applications in the assays of environments, medicines, and foods.

Yuxin Sang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Kun Wang: Methodology, Data curation. Xiangping Kong: Investigation, Resources. Fansheng Cheng: Formal analysis, Resources. Chuan Zhou: Methodology, Resources. Weina Li: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from NSFC (21403122, 32072292), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2021MC072), and High-level Science Foundation of Qingdao Agricultural University (6631405). We thank the Central Laboratory of QAU for the TEM test.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131780.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous determination of tetracycline, oxytetracycline, and 4-epitetracycline in milk by high-performance liquid chromatography
Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.W. Fritz, Y. Zuo, Simultaneous determination of tetracycline, oxytetracycline, and 4-epitetracycline in milk by high-performance liquid chromatography, Food Chem. 105 (2007) 1297–1301.

Food Control

J. Hazard. Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-enhanced fluorescence detection and degradation of tetracycline by silver nanoparticle-encapsulated halloysite nano-lumen

Mater. 342 (2018) 158–165.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Weina Li received her B. S. degree in Applied Chemistry from Southwest University in 2008 and obtained her Ph. D. in Chemistry at Tsinghua University in 2013, under the supervision of Prof. Guangtao Li and Prof. Yong Ju. She then joined the College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences at Qingdao Agricultural University in 2013 as a lecturer and an Associate Professor in 2017. Her research interest focuses on the construction of functional nanostructured materials and the application in the assays of environments, medicines, and foods
